message.content:
Find and read the request-mocking-protocol package files in /Users/duhl/git/ui to understand:
1. The `createHandler` function signature in the msw integration - what does the callback receive and what should it return?
2. Any browser-specific examples or documentation
3. How the MockClient works and what headers it sets

Look in node_modules, packages directory, or anywhere the package might live. Start with glob patterns like "**/request-mocking-protocol/**/*.{ts,js,mjs}" and read the relevant source files.

content.text: Good! The package is in node_modules. Now let me read the key source files to understand the `createHandler` function, browser-specific examples, and MockClient:
Excellent! Now let me read more about the transport utilities and protocol schemas:
Let me also check the request-matcher utilities and response-builder to get a complete picture:
Let me also check the response schema and look at how it's used in the current codebase:
Perfect! Now let me check how this is used in the tests and look at the package.json for the protocol definition:

content.text: Now let me check the request-matcher implementation to understand the full picture:

content.content: /Users/duhl/git/ui/node_modules/request-mocking-protocol/README.md

1→# Request Mocking Protocol 2→ 3→[![lint](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/lint.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/lint.yaml) 4→[![test](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/test.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/test.yaml) 5→[![npm version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/request-mocking-protocol)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/request-mocking-protocol) 6→[![license](https://img.shields.io/npm/l/request-mocking-protocol)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/blob/main/LICENSE) 7→ 8→Request Mocking Protocol (RMP) is designed for declarative mocking of HTTP requests. It provides JSON schemas for capturing requests and building responses. The schemas can be serialized and transmitted over the network, enabling both client-side and server-side mocking (e.g., in React Server Components). 9→ 10→## Features 11→ 12→- [**Server-side mocking**](#how-it-works): Apply mocks on the server by transmitting them via a custom HTTP header. 13→- [**Per-test mock isolation**](#test-runner-integration): Define mocks within each test and run the entire suite in parallel against the same app instance. 14→- [**Modern test runner support**](#test-runner-integration): Seamlessly integrates with **Playwright**, **Cypress**, and any custom test runner. 15→- [**Framework-agnostic interceptors**](#framework-integration): Use built-in interceptors for **Next.js**, **Astro**, or integrate with any other framework. 16→- [**Flexible request matching**](#request-matching): Match requests by exact URL, wildcards, query parameters, headers, or body content. 17→- [**Parameter substitution**](#parameter-substitution): Dynamically replace route or query parameters in mock responses using `{{ }}` syntax. 18→- [**Response patching**](#response-patching): Fetch real API responses and override only what you need, keeping mocks in sync with backend changes. 19→- [**Intuitive API**](#api): Provides a first-class `MockClient` for setting up the mocks. 20→- [**Debug-friendly**](#debugging): Add `debug: true` to any mock and get a nicely formatted breakdown of the mocking process. 21→ 22→## How it works 23→ 24→![How RMP works](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d274ef54-cabe-45fe-9684-5fd6dc0d626f) 25→ 26→1. The test runner declares a request mock in the JSON format. 27→2. The mock is attached to the webpage navigation request inside a custom HTTP header. 28→3. The application server reads the header and applies the mock to outgoing API calls. 29→4. The page is rendered with data from the mocked response. 30→ 31→Check out the [Concepts](#concepts) and [Limitations](#limitations) for more details. 32→ 33→## Installation 34→``` 35→npm i -D request-mocking-protocol 36→``` 37→ 38→## Test-runner Integration 39→ 40→RMP is designed to work seamlessly with popular test runners like Playwright and Cypress, and can also be integrated with custom runners. 41→ 42→Each test defines its own mocks using a [`MockClient`](#mockclient) class. Mocks are not shared across tests, enabling **per-test mock isolation** and **full parallelization**. 43→ 44→### Playwright 45→ 46→1. Set up a custom fixture `mockServerRequest`: 47→ ```ts 48→ import { test as base } from '@playwright/test'; 49→ import { MockClient } from 'request-mocking-protocol'; 50→ 51→ export const test = base.extend<{ mockServerRequest: MockClient }>({ 52→ mockServerRequest: async ({ context }, use) => { 53→ const mockClient = new MockClient(); 54→ mockClient.onChange = async (headers) => context.setExtraHTTPHeaders(headers); 55→ await use(mockClient); 56→ }, 57→ }); 58→ ``` 59→ 60→2. Use `mockServerRequest` in test to define server-side mocks: 61→ ```ts 62→ test('my test', async ({ page, mockServerRequest }) => { 63→ // set up server-side mock 64→ await mockServerRequest.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 65→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 66→ }); 67→ 68→ // navigate to the page 69→ await page.goto('/'); 70→ 71→ // assert page content according to mock 72→ await expect(page).toContainText('John Smith'); 73→ }); 74→ ``` 75→ 76→Check out [`MockClient`](#mockclient) API for other methods. 77→ 78→### Cypress 79→ 80→1. Add a custom command `mockServerRequest` in support files, see example [mock-server-request.js](examples/astro-cypress/cypress/support/mock-server-request.js). 81→ 82→2. Use the custom command to define mocks: 83→ ```js 84→ it('shows list of users', () => { 85→ // set up server-side mock 86→ cy.mockServerRequest('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 87→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 88→ }); 89→ 90→ // navigate to the page 91→ cy.visit('/'); 92→ 93→ // assert page content according to mock 94→ cy.get('li').first().should('have.text', 'John Smith'); 95→ }); 96→ ``` 97→ 98→### Custom 99→ 100→You can integrate RMP with any test runner. It requires two steps: 101→ 102→1. Use the `MockClient` class to define mocks. 103→2. Attach `mockClient.headers` to the navigation request. 104→ 105→## Framework Integration 106→ 107→On the server side, you should set up an [interceptor](#interceptors) to catch the requests and apply your mocks. 108→ 109→### Next.js (App router) 110→ 111→Add the following code to the [instrumentation.ts](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/instrumentation) file: 112→ 113→Add the following code to the top level `layout.tsx`: 114→```ts 115→// app/layout.tsx 116→import { headers } from 'next/headers'; 117→ 118→if (process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs' && process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') { 119→ const { setupFetchInterceptor } = await import('request-mocking-protocol/fetch'); 120→ setupFetchInterceptor(() => headers()); 121→} 122→ 123→// ... 124→``` 125→ 126→> [!NOTE] 127→> Apply interceptor only in `nodejs` runtime. 128→ 129→> [!IMPORTANT] 130→> Don't load interceptor inside [instrumentation.ts](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/instrumentation), as it will be cleared in dev server after re-compilation. 131→ 132→### Astro 133→See [astro.config.ts](examples/astro-cypress/astro.config.ts) in the astro-cypress example. 134→ 135→### Custom 136→ 137→You can write an interceptor for any framework. It requires two steps: 138→ 139→1. Read the HTTP headers of the incoming request. 140→2. Capture outgoing HTTP requests. 141→ 142→Check out the reference implementations in the [src/interceptors](src/interceptors) directory. 143→ 144→## Request Matching 145→ 146→RMP offers flexible matching options to ensure your mocks are applied exactly when you need them: 147→ 148→- **Exact URL matching**: Match requests by providing a full URL string. 149→ ```ts 150→ await mockClient.GET('https://api.example.com/users', { body: [] }); 151→ ``` 152→ 153→- **Wildcard matching**: Use wildcards with [URLPattern](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/URLPattern)-style syntax. 154→ ```ts 155→ await mockClient.GET('https://api.example.com/users/*', { body: [] }); 156→ ``` 157→ 158→- **Regular expression matching**: Match requests using JavaScript regular expressions. 159→ ```ts 160→ await mockClient.GET(/\/users\/\d+$/, { body: {} }); 161→ ``` 162→ 163→- **Query parameter matching**: Match specific query parameters for more targeted mocks. 164→ ```ts 165→ await mockClient.GET({ 166→ url: 'https://api.example.com/users', 167→ query: { role: 'admin' }, 168→ }, { body: [] }); 169→ ``` 170→ 171→- **Method-based matching**: Explicitly define the HTTP method (`GET`, `POST`, etc.) to avoid accidental matches. 172→ ```ts 173→ await mockClient.POST('https://api.example.com/users', { status: 201 }); 174→ ``` 175→ 176→- **Schema matching**: Use full request schemas to match by method, URL, query, and optionally enable `debug` mode for inspection. 177→ ```ts 178→ await mockClient.GET({ 179→ method: 'GET', 180→ url: 'https://api.example.com/users', 181→ query: { active: 'true' }, 182→ debug: true, 183→ }, { body: [] }); 184→ ``` 185→ 186→## Parameter Substitution 187→ 188→You can define route parameters in the URL pattern and use them in the response: 189→ 190→```ts 191→await mockClient.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/:id', { 192→ body: { 193→ id: '{{ id:number }}', 194→ name: 'User {{ id }}', 195→ } 196→}); 197→``` 198→ 199→The request: 200→``` 201→GET https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1 202→``` 203→will be mocked with the response: 204→```js 205→{ 206→ id: 1, 207→ name: 'User 1', 208→} 209→``` 210→ 211→## Response Patching 212→ 213→Response patching allows to make a real request, but modify parts of the response for the testing purposes. 214→RMP supports response patching by providing the `bodyPatch` key in the response schema: 215→ 216→```ts 217→await mockClient.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 218→ bodyPatch: { 219→ '[0].address.city': 'New York', 220→ }, 221→}); 222→``` 223→The final response will contain actual and modified data: 224→```diff 225→[ 226→ { 227→ "id": 1, 228→ "name": "Leanne Graham", 229→ "address": { 230→- "city": "Gwenborough", 231→+ "city": "New York", 232→ ... 233→ } 234→ } 235→ ... 236→] 237→``` 238→This technique is particularly useful to keep your tests in sync with actual API responses, while maintaining test stability and logic. 239→ 240→The `bodyPatch` contains object in a form: 241→``` 242→{ 243→ [path.to.property]: new value 244→} 245→``` 246→`path.to.property` uses dot-notation, evaluated with [lodash.set](https://lodash.com/docs/4.17.15#set). 247→ 248→## Debugging 249→ 250→You can debug the mocking process by providing `debug: true` option to either request or response schema: 251→ 252→```ts 253→await mockClient.GET( 254→ { 255→ url: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', 256→ query: { 257→ foo: 'bar', 258→ }, 259→ debug: true, // <-- use debugging 260→ }, 261→ { 262→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 263→ }, 264→); 265→``` 266→When applying this mock, the server console with output the following: 267→ 268→ 269→ 270→## Concepts 271→ 272→### Request Schema 273→ 274→The request schema is a serializable object that defines parameters for matching a request. 275→ 276→Example: 277→```js 278→{ 279→ method: 'GET', 280→ url: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', 281→ query: { 282→ foo: 'bar' 283→ } 284→} 285→``` 286→This schema will match the request: 287→``` 288→GET https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users?foo=bar 289→``` 290→ 291→[Full schema definition.](src/protocol/request-schema.ts) 292→ 293→### Response Schema 294→ 295→The response schema is a serializable object that defines how to build the mocked response. 296→ 297→Example: 298→```js 299→{ 300→ status: 200, Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
